speaker: interviewer
text: The date is September 20, 1995. Survivor, Ellen Kaidanow, maiden name Leviatin. Interviewer, Naomi Rappaport. The city is New Rochelle. The state is New York. The country, USA, and the language is English. My name is Naomi Rappaport, and today is September 20, 1995. I am conducting an interview with Ellen Kaidanow, maiden name Leviatin, in New Rochelle, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mrs. Kaidanow, for welcoming us to your home and telling your story to the Shoah Foundation. I'd like to start with asking you to give us your name and spell it, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ellen Kaidanow. And the spelling is E-L-L-E-N, for Ellen, Kaidanow, K-A-I-D-A-N-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: At birth my name was Shifra Leviatin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell them, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's S-H-I-F-R-A. Leviatin is L-E-V-I-A-T-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not pre-war.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --do you have any other names during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, during the war, being on the Aryan papers, my name was Marusia Duvzinski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-R-U-S-I-A. And Duvzinski is D-U-V-Z-I-N-S-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is June 15, 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am now 59.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city-- in which city were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Dubno, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Dubno, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-U-B-N-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to start the interview with your early life and your memories of your life in Dubno as a child, and a bit of the history of your family. What were your parent's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Esther, and my father's name was Joseph.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mother's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's maiden name was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they always live into Dubno?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have grandparents that lived there as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not remember. I only remember one grandparent, and that was my father's mother by the name of Sarah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the home, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of your home? What did it look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that it was very beautiful. My parents had a candy store where they sold candy and soda. It was very small, but they apparently made a very nice living, because we lived on a beautiful street called Panienska Ulica, which means Panienska Street. And I remember that we had a very big house with an awful lot of land with a gate around it. And they used to have strawberries growing. And I remember running around, you know, with neighbors' kids in the back yard. And in the house, I don't have a visual memory of everything. But somehow I remember the dining room having a very big table and a very nice buffet. And really, that's all I remember out of, you know, all the furnishings. But I know that it was very nice, because just the street itself tells you that it was a very beautiful area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a self-contained home, or was it an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a home, a self-contained home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many other rooms were there in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, boy. I truly don't have an idea. Because somehow I do remember there, there was a bedroom where my parents slept. And I think that we had beds in the same room, as I remember it. And there were other rooms, but I don't remember them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else lived in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father, of course. And I had two sisters, an older one and a younger one. I was the middle one. And there was a brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have help in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have somebody--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Because my parents both worked in that very small store that I described to you, we had a woman that was with us, at least all my life. And I truly knew her better than I know my mother. And her name was Lena Duzinska. She was a Ukrainian woman that lived with us, always as far as I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you remember your dining room very well. And what was kept in the breakfront or the cabinets?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: --the silverware. And the reason that I think I remember it is because I remember taking some candy out of there and being punished, not by my mother, but by my older sister. Like, you know, she was yelling at me for doing that. And so maybe that's why I remember that buffet. What else was in there, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other ritual objects around the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember any. I, I don't remember specific items.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how old-- how large Dubno was? How large was the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they were 12,000 Jews, but I don't how large the city itself was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the street on which you lived, were there may Jews or were there others?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that many. It was a mixed area. There were a lot of Czech people living there. I remember a beautiful park, like across the street from us. I remember playing in that park. Really a beautiful, gorgeous park. And that's about it. Not too many Jews. I think it was a mixed area.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the-- in Dubno itself, what was the general-- what kind of a city was it? Did it have an economic base of some sort?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know that it was a cultural city, because they had their-- they used to say, a Dubno [NON-ENGLISH], which was a very learned man that lived there. And also they had, I believe, they had typing, you know, like the books, printing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Printing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a book center?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: In terms of the back of house where you said there was a garden, what else was-- what was grown in there? Were you growing food that you ate?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was just for your pleasure. And I do remember that, like, opposite the house, there was, like, a very big cellar where they used to save for the wintertime potatoes, you know. And every once in a while, we kids would run in there. It was very dark and very deep, like a tunnel.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was part of your own house?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on the grounds of the house, not inside the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was separate, completely separate from the house. I remember it, like, you know, through a dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who cooked in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lena.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lena cooked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the home Kosher?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, at least not that I know. I didn't know anything about Kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- your mother did not do any kind of housework at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess she supervised. You know, I just don't have a memory of it. I know it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you were very young, actually, at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any life cycle events in your home-- births, weddings, funerals?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about Jewish holidays? Do you remember those?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think Passover I remember mostly, but not as a ritual, I have to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you-- what do you remember about Passover?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just that it was Passover. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember meals, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember meals.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whom-- who were your friends? Who did you play with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe, if I try to visualize our home, sort of to the left of our house, there was another family. I think their name was [NON-ENGLISH]. I'm not even sure of that, but I believe so. And they had children. I remember playing with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember what kind of playing you did, what kind of games you played?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- just, you know, going to the park and normal children playing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend school at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I did not. I think that that year I was supposed to go into school when, you know, the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your sister attend school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she did, the older one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school did she go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember books in the house or newspapers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just remember that I used to like, you know, writing and things like this. I do remember paper, but not books. I don't remember books.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language was spoken in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Yiddish and Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Lena, the woman who took care of you, where did she sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't remember. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- when were you were you aware of changes in your life as a result of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first change, and it wasn't really a change, but looking back on my memory, I remember somebody coming to our house. I think it was an uncle. And he said to my mother, Esther, pack up the children. The war is coming closer to our town. Let's run away to Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, what? You're crazy. I'm going to leave all of this? Like, she was very attached to her things. She said, you know, how long could the war last? No, I'm not going with you. And that was my first thing that I remember, just standing there and overhearing it. It wasn't said to me, but somehow I remember that. And when were you first totally aware of the war? When did life change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Life changed, I-- why I remember that it was Passover, I don't know, whether they talked about it or what. I remember, it's going to sound funny to you, but for some reason, I remember myself sitting on the potty, like in a little alcove. And a cousin who lived more in the Jewish area-- and if I remember correctly, her name was Sasha, something like that. And she came in and she said to my parents that, do you know that they created a ghetto, and we'll all have to move to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in 1939, or was this in 1941?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of the-- were there Germans in Dubno in 1939 as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I not-- I don't remember seeing them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were-- do you remember Russians in Dubno in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, what happened was I-- when I was born, it was Poland. And the Russians did come in in '39. But I didn't see soldiers or things like that. I just know, you know, they were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any discussion in the house itself, other than your uncle who said, let's go to Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the ghetto get established?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was either the end of sometimes in '40-- end of '41 or '42, maybe '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember moving to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I remember, yes. I remembered that we packed up. We locked up the house. And suddenly I found myself in a very tremendous-- well, tremendous to me-- place with wooden plaques bordered around it. That's-- made the ghetto.  In other words, they, like a-- not a gate--

speaker: interviewer
text: A fence?

speaker: interviewee
text: A fence, a wooden fence. Very tall. Very tall wooden fence.

speaker: interviewer
text: They put it up, or it was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was put up. And what happened, in our case, that grandmother that I say to you that I remember her, Sarah, her name was, she lived there in that area. Because what they did was they created the ghetto where mostly Jews were already. And only those that were outside of it went in. Because the ones that were there were there already.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she had a home there, a house. It was not a single house like ours. It had, like, three families in there. I think a brother of my father's lived in the back, and then sister in the front, and maybe another brother, whom I don't remember, also lived in that house. So they made room for us, like in the back of the house, just for our family. Do you remember what you took with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you pack anything of your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have any memory of any such things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I get there? I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's possible that we walked. Yeah. Yeah, we walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was the ghetto from where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it's not so big, so I don't think it was that far. I can't even estimate it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you all go together, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we all walked together, yes. Because, see, prior to that, I think before they established the ghetto, Jews were-- properties were confiscated. We had to wear, I remember distinctly, yellow stars. That I remember, all of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you wear the yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the back, like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even little children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even little children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it say anything on the yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think Jude.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what else were you subjected to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Prior to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yea.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father still able to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the candy store, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: You closed-- you say you closed the door and you left to go to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any discussions, any moods, any feeling things?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I do remember moods, not discussions. Because you have to remember that in those days, children were not exactly cued into everything. So everything had to be sort of by osmosis.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember the sadness, and I remember them walking around, you know, like this with their hands. And it was just very, very depressing and very sad everyone, you know. My father would talk to my mother quietly. And what should we do? What should we do? I remember that. When-- did you hide anything before you left? Do you know if they hid stuff?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in that home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do? They had--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto they hid? But they had--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- I don't know what happened to the things. I don't know. I think they just locked up and walked out with the packages, whatever they could take in their hands, and thinking that they would be back or, you know, that it would last maybe a couple of weeks or months and they'll be back.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you were very young, but was there any knowledge of what had been going on in other parts of Poland and Nazi-ism?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would not know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were, you were too young.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I only remember what was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you got to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember. I remember being excited, because my grandma was there, whom I loved very much. And being closer with everyone else, there were more children to play with. So I remember that. I also remember that my aunt was married to her husband. [NON-ENGLISH] his name was. And I remember that he was-- he had been in, I think, in Lwow to visit family of his. And when he came back that day-- because I remember my parents saying that on the street, like many times, if you were on the street, you would get killed immediately.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, like, if we knew, if somebody said the Germans are coming, everybody went into their houses. And he was killed in that first [NON-ENGLISH]. That I remember. The Germans entered the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --or he was killed outside of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside the ghetto. Inside the ghetto. That was my first thing that I knew, the person, the first person that I ever knew of that got-- that was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- do you remember what happened after he was killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, the fear was always there, of course. People disappeared. My father was constricted to work, some kind of work. I don't know what kind of work, but you know, leave early in the morning and  come--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was taken? He was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken to work. Like, they used to-- not just him, you know, a lot of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember her being taken anywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he came back at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Came back at night, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I asked, do you remember what happened after your uncle was shot, was there a funeral? Was there a burial? Was--

speaker: interviewee
text: If there was, they didn't let children come to it. So I did not go to any funeral at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember whether they sat shiva or whether--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They didn't, because it wasn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there-- were there rituals that they were following at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. If anything, there was a little more religion in the ghetto. Because you know, if I think back now, I remember people coming to the house and they were davening. I didn't know what it was all about though. It was meaningless to me. But I do remember that on certain occasions. I don't know which occasions.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you sleep in that ghetto house? Were you all in one room, or did you have more space?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think there we also had, like, two rooms. You know, it's, it's really a terrible thing, you see. Because I feel that my age group is sort of more pathetic, or I don't know what word to use, because I have no memory. I have no background. I have no foundation. It's like I'm in the air flying around in the air, you know, like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was young. But I just feel that I don't-- I have nothing to ground me down because of that. Because the questions you're asking, I feel stupid because I just don't know any of the answers.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were very young so your memories are those of a --

speaker: interviewee
text: I just was completely unaware.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of a, of a 5-year-old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or I, I don't think I blocked it out, because I just don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you remember where you ate and who cooked in the house and whether there was food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I know that there was very little food. And I do have to tell you that this Lena never deserted us. And I remember that they found like one of the plaques from the gate that I told you around the ghetto was sort of a little bit open. And I do remember that she used to push through food for us, and my father would go and get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did -- she brought her own food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yea, from wherever she got it. She would bring us food and push it under that one single plank or board or whatever you want to call it. And she would send in food. I don't remember being hungry, because food was never, like, I don't remember what I ate. It's funny.

speaker: interviewer
text: How, in your mind or in your vision, how big was that fence, not in height, but in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite big,

speaker: interviewer
text: --circumference?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite big. Very big. Because I remember that we were sort of on the edge, but there was a lake. A [NON-ENGLISH] is a lake?

speaker: interviewer
text: A [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], yea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is a river.

speaker: interviewee
text: A river. Because I remember going to that river. I don't know, to wash laundry--

speaker: interviewer
text: Within the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Within the ghetto. There was like water. For some reason I remember water. Further away from us, quite a bit far, so it was quite large. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do-- you say you played with children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, with the immediate near us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any kind of organized activity in--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never went to any organized activity. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hardly remember her.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in that ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's another thing. The Dubno ghetto was not that long.  I believe like nine months or so, and then they liquidated it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say that-- you said that there were [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, two that I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What can you tell me about the [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the first one I already told you. That was when whoever was in the street was killed. And I do remember a second [NON-ENGLISH]. See, the Germans were not in the ghetto at all times. What they did was they took some people, young men, and there were gates. I think they were, on both sides, gates, like, you know. And they had Jewish policeman attend those gates. And whenever the Germans came, somebody from somewhere, I don't know who it was, whether they had people on the outside, I do remember that they would warn us. And they would say, [NON-ENGLISH], they're coming. And once they said, they're coming, everybody ran either to hide. Which we happen to have had-- my father was a carpenter, so they built behind a closet what they call [NON-ENGLISH], which was like a hiding place.

speaker: interviewer
text: A bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: A bunker, exactly. And many a times we would go into that bunker when these things happen. They didn't seem to go into houses at that time. But, like, they would just storm into the ghetto. And sometimes I think maybe it's true, maybe not. But I swear to you that at one time, I saw a German take a young kid. They had barrels, you know, for rain water, filled up full of rainwater. He just dropped the kid in there. And that I saw with my own eyes. And we just ran into the house. And once it was over, they had enough of blood sucking, sort of to speak, they left. So they were not, like, constantly in the ghetto, the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- were you familiar with the Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just knew that they were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about the Judenrat? Were you familiar with-- was that discussed at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, I just--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go back to the bunker, that you say your father was a carpenter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what it looked like and how big it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was quite something, as I remember it. And I remember they had some kind of pills, like to dilute urine to water if necessary. And of course they put potatoes in and all kinds of dried goods, dried bread, and things like that. And I remember it being very narrow as we walked in. We had to crouch to walk in. But then it sort of opened up. And they had, like, beds, like you would have bunk, bunk beds now. Well, they weren't exactly bunk beds, but narrow ones like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How, how big would you say it was in, you know, in terms of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say maybe like my kitchen, 10 by 10, something, maybe 8 by 8.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people could get into it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let's say, 4. I guess about 8 or 10 people, not more. 8.

speaker: interviewer
text: Standing or sitting?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that area, I think it was standing. I really think it was standing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the bunker built only for your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Immediate and the uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your extended family?

speaker: interviewee
text: That uncle Motl that lived in back of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody else ever want to be in your bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember it. See, by the time they hid in the bunker for good, I wasn't there anymore, which I'll tell you about later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have to go into the bunker to hide because of an [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I told you. It was like a door that sort of opened up.  It looked like a closet. And then you had to crawl into there, and then it suddenly opened up into a room, maybe 8 by 8.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- what was in front of the closet?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, when I say a closet, it's funny, because their closets are like free standing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yea. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was what--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was kept in that closet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Linen. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: To make it look like a closet, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yea. I don't know, linen probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, did you have to practice to get into that bunker? Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: One. Yes, we did. Because that's how I know how it was. Because then I never went in there again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you practice? How long did it take to get everybody in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Just a couple of minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about silence?

speaker: interviewee
text: It had to be fast. Silent.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the rules? What were the rules?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolute silence.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were a small child, and your sister was even smaller.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other little children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No other little children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they keep you quiet?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we were just quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, frankly, you know, as I said to you, you see, I wasn't there when the full-- when they liquidated the ghetto. That's when they really went into hiding. Because for the [NON-ENGLISH], you only went in for an hour or so until they were finished. When you felt that the Germans left, you came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the kind you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many times were you in that bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once, I remember, only once.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a radio in the bunker?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't-- I don't know. I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember who was in the bunker with you that particular one time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meaning?

speaker: interviewee
text: The father, mother, sisters, and the-- I think the uncle and one of the daughters was there too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't remember her going in. In fact, she didn't want to go in, which I'll tell you about later.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK We're going to change it.
